title: Cookie Chain — Introduction
content:
Cookie Chain — Introduction

Cookie Chain is a community-run Solana fork. It started as a Solana fork. The original group of community devs built a thriving chain and ecosystem. When the core team's vision stopped aligning with the community, the community forked the fork and kept shipping.

Today, Cookie Chain is operated by an expanding multi-validator set with COOK as the native asset. Real validators. Real governance. Real shipping.

What makes Cookie Chain different:
- Solana compatible: SPL tokens, programs, and tooling work out of the box.
- Community operated: no foundation gate, no single operator.
- Multi-sig bridge: COOK moves between Solana and Cookie Chain through an m-of-n community multi-sig — no single key controls the vault.
- Sub-second finality: ~1s block times with Solana-style consensus.
- Minimal fees: fees paid in COOK, priced for builders and users, not extractors.
- Dirt-cheap program deploys: deploying a program on Cookie Chain costs next to nothing compared to Solana mainnet.

title: Cookie Chain — Getting Started
content:
Cookie Chain — Getting Started

Connect to Cookie Chain in minutes with the Solana CLI, Nightly wallet, and the community RPC.

1. Point the Solana CLI at Cookie Chain:
   solana config set --url https://rpc.cookiescan.io

2. Check the connection:
   solana cluster-version

3. Create or import a wallet:
   solana-keygen new --outfile ~/.config/solana/id.json

Wallet support includes Nightly. Install from nightly.app and connect to Cookie Chain with the community RPC endpoint: https://rpc.cookiescan.io

4. Get COOK: Bridge COOK from Solana through the community multi-sig bridge at bridge.cookiescan.io

5. Explore:
   - Block explorer: cookiescan.io
   - Deployed programs: cookiescan.io/programs
   - Validator set: cookiescan.io/validators

title: Cookie Chain — Wallets
content:
Cookie Chain — Wallets

Cookie Chain supports standard Solana-compatible wallets, including browser wallets, mobile wallets, and hardware-backed flows.

Recommended wallet: Nightly — fully supported on Cookie Chain. Install from nightly.app.

Connect to Cookie Chain:
- Open your wallet network settings and choose custom RPC or custom SVM network.
- RPC: https://rpc.cookiescan.io
- WebSocket: https://wss.cookiescan.io
- Bridge: bridge.cookiescan.io

Quick checklist:
- Back up your seed phrase offline.
- Verify URLs before signing transactions.
- Use separate wallets for high-value storage and daily dApp use.

title: Cookie Chain FAQ — Cookienet vs Cookiechain
content:
Cookie Chain FAQ — Cookienet vs Cookiechain

Cookienet and Cookie Chain are synonymous — they refer to the same network.

The name 'Cookienet' is an informal community shorthand for the Cookie Chain network. Both names refer to the same SVM chain running with COOK as its native asset. Official documentation uses 'Cookie Chain' as the primary name.

title: Cookie Chain — Architecture & Consensus
content:
Cookie Chain — Architecture & Consensus

Cookie Chain inherits SVM architecture with PoH, Tower BFT, Turbine, Gulf Stream, Sealevel, and pipelining.

Proof of History (PoH): A verifiable delay function acts as a cryptographic clock and orders events before consensus runs.

Tower BFT: A PoH-optimized PBFT variant lets validators vote on the PoH sequence, reducing messaging overhead and supporting sub-second finality.

Turbine: block propagation via randomized shredding.
Gulf Stream: mempool-less transaction forwarding directly to the upcoming leader.
Sealevel: parallel program execution across CPU cores.
Pipelining: staged transaction validation for steady throughput.

Block time & finality:
- Target block time: ~80-140 ms
- Finality is sub-second under normal conditions.
- Consensus is produced by an expanding set of independent validators.

title: Cookie Chain — Validators
content:
Cookie Chain — Validators

Cookie Chain is operated by an expanding multi-validator set. Validator onboarding is open to qualified community members.

Validators secure the chain through stake and produce blocks using Solana-style consensus (Tower BFT + PoH). No single validator or foundation controls the chain.

View the current validator set at: cookiescan.io/validators

Validator incentives are funded through pump.fun creator fees from the Solana-side token launch, routed back into the ecosystem.

title: Cookie Chain — Community Multi-sig Bridge
content:
Cookie Chain — Community Multi-sig Bridge

COOK moves between Solana and Cookie Chain through a community-operated multi-sig. It is a lock/unlock bridge — no single party can move bridge funds unilaterally.

Flow: Solana to Cookie Chain:
- Lock on Solana: deposit COOK into bridge vault on Solana mainnet.
- Unlock on Cookie Chain: once the threshold is met, the multi-sig authorizes the corresponding asset to unlock on Cookie Chain.

Bridge vault address: G3mm95M4ns7mk8oseWGJnirvgyMahMz3vZEUhdJn8oGX (Vault 0)
Bridge frontend: bridge.cookiescan.io
Multisig transparency dashboard: sig.cookiechain.wtf/community

Token roles:
- sCOOK: Solana-side COOK (created on pump.fun)
- cCOOK: Cookie Chain-side COOK (the native COOK)
- Locked COOK: acquired locked allocation representing 27% of the supply

The plan: create pump.fun token on Solana (sCOOK), acquire the locked 27% COOK allocation, unlock bridge-out for locked holders, then operate the 1:1 sCOOK ↔ cCOOK relayer bridge under community multisig custody.

title: Cookie Chain — Developer Guide
content:
Cookie Chain — Developer Guide

Cookie Chain is SVM-compatible. Anchor, native Rust, and standard Solana client SDKs work with no changes beyond pointing at the Cookie Chain RPC.

Endpoints:
- HTTP RPC: https://rpc.cookiescan.io
- WebSocket: https://wss.cookiescan.io

JavaScript/TypeScript:
  import { Connection } from '@solana/web3.js';
  const connection = new Connection('https://rpc.cookiescan.io', 'confirmed');

Anchor.toml:
  [provider]
  cluster = 'https://rpc.cookiescan.io'

Deploy a program:
  solana program deploy ./target/deploy/my_program.so

Builder quick path:
- Set CLI/SDK to https://rpc.cookiescan.io
- Deploy programs with Solana CLI, validate on Cookiescan
- Submit ecosystem programs at cookiescan.io/submit-project

Developer tools: cookiescan.io/developer-tools
API playground: api.cookiescan.io/playground

title: Cookie Chain — FAQ & Governance
content:
Cookie Chain — FAQ & Governance

Cookie Chain governance is off-chain-first and community-led. Major decisions — including bridge signer rotation, validator set policy, treasury spend, and protocol-level upgrades — are discussed openly and ratified by the multi-sig and validator operator set.

Principles:
- No single point of control: bridge vault, core infrastructure, and treasury all sit behind m-of-n signer sets.
- Open participation: validator onboarding and program listings are open to qualified community members.
- Progressive decentralization: operator and signer sets expand over time.

Where decisions happen:
- Community channels (Discord and X) for proposals, discussion, and RFCs.
- Multi-sig signer ceremonies for on-chain ratification.
- Validator operator coordination for upgrade timing.

Common questions:
Q: What wallet should I use? A: Nightly wallet (nightly.app) — fully supported.
Q: What is the RPC endpoint? A: https://rpc.cookiescan.io
Q: How do I get COOK? A: Bridge from Solana via bridge.cookiescan.io or swap on ecosystem DEXs.
Q: Is Cookie Chain the same as Cookienet? A: Yes — cookienet and cookiechain are synonymous names for the same network.

title: Introducing the Cookie Recipe — Cookie Chain Origin Story
content:
Introducing the Cookie Recipe 🍪
Cookie Chain belongs to the third category.
Tech (Tekh) is the Meme, Lore, Cookies.
Cookie Chain was born from frustration, survival, and belief.
Not belief in marketing. Belief in builders.
Before Cookie Chain existed, there was Gorbagana… A chaotic experimental ecosystem forked from Solana. It attracted developers because it felt raw, fast, and open. People built strange things. Fun things. Real things.
But eventually the experiment stopped feeling decentralized. The community slowly realized something painful: The ecosystem depended too heavily on a centralized operational structure. One man’s control of the chain. Many empty promises. One hand gets all of the money that chain gets. And then the chain halted... Not once. Twice. For a combined total of nearly 96 hours. During these outages, the community received almost no communication. Builders were left in uncertainty while the ecosystem stood frozen.
At the same time, many community developers felt abandoned. The people building applications, infrastructure, and liquidity for the ecosystem were rarely supported, while chain revenues continuously flowed toward the core structure itself.
That moment changed everything.
Cookie Chain was born from the idea that ecosystems should belong to communities, not gatekeepers.
How Did Cookie Chain Idea Come to Life?
When Gorbagana halted, something important became clear: A blockchain is not its servers. A blockchain is not its branding. A blockchain is its people.
Fortunately, the community still possessed something powerful: The snapshot. The state survived. The builders survived. And that meant the ecosystem itself could survive.
At that moment, community developers decided they had seen enough. Enough silence. Enough dependency. Enough centralized control over an ecosystem built by its users.
So the builders chose to fork the chain themselves and create something different: A truly community-friendly chain where infrastructure, validators, developers, and treasury systems belong to the ecosystem. Not to rulers.
That chain became Cookie Chain.
From Snapshot to Survival
Cookie Chain is forked directly from Gorbagana, which itself was forked from Solana. A fork of a fork.
But this is more than copied code. It is preserved history. When Cookie Chain launches, users will discover something important: Their assets already exist there. All user balances and states from Gorbagana are cloned directly into Cookie Chain. Think of it almost like a massive community airdrop. A second chance. A new culture. A fresh beginning.
After the fork, Cookie Chain assets become fully independent from Gorbagana assets. Markets, liquidity, and prices evolve separately. The histories are shared. The future is not.
Joining Cookie Chain is simple: Users only need to switch their RPC endpoint to:
https://rpc.cookiescan.io
State Cloning & The Community Airdrop
Cookie Chain users are not starting from zero. The ecosystem history matters. Wallets matter. Communities matter. Builders matter.
That is why the state itself was preserved. The chain remembers who participated. Many users can think of Cookie Chain as receiving a parallel ecosystem version of their existing Gorbagana assets. Same history. Different future.
The Community Vaults
Cookie Chain is structured around community-controlled treasury systems rather than centralized ownership. The primary governance structure is controlled through a Squads Protocol Multisig:
BQVAdo6Ls2rYAggzXu38peyYg8BtQASUn6bnm9FfS5Ab
Within this multisig exists:

Vault 0 (Bridge Vault): G3mm95M4ns7mk8oseWGJnirvgyMahMz3vZEUhdJn8oGX — contains the non-circulating cCOOK supply intended for bridge infrastructure.
Vault 1 (Cookie Jar): 568tU9FMksJDxjkLBjWisSA4J4C5uPH87NCCkyREwrxe — Protocols can voluntarily contribute a portion of their on-chain revenue into this shared ecosystem vault to fund developers, validators, infrastructure, and builders.

Even the future Pump.fun creator fees generated by sCOOK trading on Solana will flow into protected multisig-controlled treasury systems dedicated entirely to community growth.
Why Cookie Chain Has No Rulers
The remaining 73% of the supply is not privately owned. It is community-controlled.
Developers Will Cook
Cookie Chain is designed for builders aka cookers:

~400ms block times
Negligible deployment costs
Minimal friction
Open bridge infrastructure available from day one

The Builders Are Moving 🍪
Cookie Chain is not being built by a single team. It is being rebuilt by an entire ecosystem.
Many original Gorbagana community developers are migrating with their projects:

Trashbin AMM → CookieSwap AMM
Dumpsack Wallet → Morsel Wallet
Trashscan → CookieScan
Bang.meme (launchpad)
TrashDAQ → Candy Shop (DEX aggregator)
CookBook (.cook domain service)
CookLock (native token vesting and locking protocol)

The ecosystem roadmap includes Public DAS Indexer infrastructure, Native Airdrop Applications, Advanced Charting systems, Perpetual Exchanges, Prediction Markets, Community-native bridge infrastructure, and Nightly Wallet integration.
The Multi-Validator Future
Cookie Chain believes chains should survive beyond individuals. Validator diversity and infrastructure resilience matter. No single baker should control the oven.
Final Words
Cookie Chain is not trying to become another polished corporate blockchain. It is something stranger. A preserved ecosystem. A forked culture. A community experiment that refused to disappear.
The chain halted. But the builders kept cooking.
Community will cook. 🍪

title: Cookiescan Trading API
content:
Cookiescan Trading API

The Cookiescan API provides programmatic access to Cookie Chain blockchain data including transactions, blocks, tokens, validators, and analytics.

API Playground: https://api.cookiescan.io/playground
Base URL: https://api.cookiescan.io

Use the API to build trading bots, indexers, dashboards, and data tools on top of Cookie Chain. The playground lets you explore endpoints interactively.

title: Cookie Bridge — Transfer COOK between Cookie Chain & Solana
content:
Cookie Bridge — Transfer COOK between Cookie Chain & Solana

The Cookie Bridge lets you move COOK between Solana and Cookie Chain through a community-operated multi-sig vault.

Bridge URL: https://bridge.cookiescan.io

How it works:
- Solana → Cookie Chain: deposit COOK into the bridge vault on Solana mainnet. Once the multi-sig threshold is met, COOK is unlocked on Cookie Chain.
- Cookie Chain → Solana: the same signer set coordinates the reverse unlock.

Bridge vault address (Vault 0): G3mm95M4ns7mk8oseWGJnirvgyMahMz3vZEUhdJn8oGX
Multisig transparency: sig.cookiechain.wtf/community

Only use the official bridge URL. Verify the URL before signing any transaction.

title: CandyShop — Cookie Chain DEX Aggregator
content:
CandyShop — Cookie Chain DEX Aggregator

CandyShop is the DEX aggregator for Cookie Chain, allowing users to swap tokens at the best available rates across liquidity sources on the network.

URL: https://swap.cookiescan.io

Use CandyShop to swap COOK and other SPL tokens on Cookie Chain. It is the primary trading interface for the Cookie Chain ecosystem.

title: Cookoven — dApp Hub for Cookie Chain
content:
Cookoven — The Hub for dApps on Cookie Chain

Cookoven is the central hub for discovering and accessing decentralized applications (dApps) built on Cookie Chain.

URL: https://cookoven.xyz

Browse the Cookie Chain ecosystem, find live programs, and explore what builders have shipped on the network. Cookoven is the go-to directory for Cookie Chain dApps and ecosystem projects.

title: Cookiequad Multisig
content:
Cookiequad Multisig
Cookiequad Multisig
Creating teams, managing members and handling proposals on-chain.
Create your team, set permissions, and manage vaults with an intuitive interface.
0
FEES
ALL
TRANSPARENT
on
COOKIE
CHAIN
Create Multisig
Manage Existing Multisig
Community Wallet
How It Works
Understand the multisig flow
1. Create a multisig with members, permissions, threshold, and optional authorities.
2. Fund a vault PDA and create proposals for transfers or configuration changes.
3. Members approve or reject proposals based on their voting permission.
4. Once a proposal is approved and any time lock has passed, an executor can run it.
What You Can Do
Core capabilities included
Create autonomous or controlled multisigs.
View members, permissions, vault balances, spending limits, and proposals.
Create vault transfer proposals and config proposals.
Approve, reject, cancel, activate, and execute proposals.
Use existing spending limits directly from the UI.
Home
Start here
Use this page to understand the app and navigate the workflow.
Create Multisig
Set up your team
Add members, choose permissions, set threshold, and create a new multisig with Backpack.
Go to creation page
Community Wallet
View Cookiequad treasury
Inspect the community multisig in read-only mode, or connect a member wallet to approve and execute allowed actions.
Go to community page
Manage Multisig
Handle proposals
Load an existing multisig PDA and manage proposals, vault transfers, members, and limits.
Go to management page

title: Cookie Chain — Break Gorbagana (Network Stress Test)
content:
Cookie Chain — Break Gorbagana (Network Stress Test)

An interactive stress-test tool on cookiescan.io that lets users hammer the Cookie Chain network with rapid RPC requests to test its performance.

Cookie Chain is a high-performance blockchain with ~1-second block times, sub-second finality, and low fees. The stress test demonstrates the network's capacity for high-throughput transactions.

URL: https://cookiescan.io/break-gorbagana

Powered by the Solana Virtual Machine (SVM) with proof-of-history consensus and parallel transaction processing.

title: Gorbagana Cash — For Builders
content:
Build On GCASH
For builders
How to migrate or deploy projects depending on whether you are coming from Trashnet or Solana.
Docs
For builders
How to migrate or deploy projects depending on whether you are coming from Trashnet or Solana.
Coming from Trashnet
If you built before the fork, migration should be simple. Your project already targets the same chain environment. In most cases, you only need to replace your old RPC URL with the Gorbagana Cash community RPC.
https://community-rpc.trashscan.io
Coming from Solana
01
Bridge tokens
Bridge a small amount of tokens from Solana to Gorbagana Cash so you can pay for transactions and deployments.
02
Point Solana CLI to GCASH
Configure your Solana CLI to use the Gorbagana Cash RPC endpoint instead of a Solana RPC endpoint.
solana config set --url https://community-rpc.trashscan.io
solana config get
03
Deploy programs
Deploy your existing Solana programs with the CLI. Rust, Anchor, or any compatible Solana program workflow should feel familiar.
solana program deploy ./target/deploy/your_program.so
# Anchor example
anchor deploy --provider.cluster https://community-rpc.trashscan.io
04
Update your dApp RPCs
In your frontend, backend, indexer, or scripts, use Gorbagana Cash RPC endpoints for reads and writes.
Gorbagana Cash RPC
Use this RPC everywhere your Solana app expects a cluster URL.
https://community-rpc.trashscan.io

title: Gorbagana Cash — Documentation Overview
content:
Docs
Read the manual. Eat the cookie.
Everything needed to understand the fork, join the network, and build on GCASH.
Docs
Introduction
The story of Gorbagana Cash, why the fork exists, and what the community is continuing.
Gorbagana Cash is a community-owned hard fork of Gorbagana. It keeps the historical continuity of the chain, but moves the future away from the old single-operator control model.
The community went through drama, instability, and uncertainty. When the original Gorbagana chain was halted by Rick, the network could have ended there. Fortunately, a backup snapshot survived.
That snapshot became the foundation for a community-led hard fork. The result is simple: same chain history, new community future. GCASH is the continuation of the state, the users, and the builders who stayed.
Core idea
Same chain history. New community future.

title: Gorbagana Cash — For Degens
content:
Use The Chain
For degens
If you know Solana, you already understand the base experience. The apps and culture are what change.
Docs
For degens
If you know Solana, you already understand the base experience. The apps and culture are what change.
Gorbagana Cash behaves like Solana at the network level. Wallet flows, transactions, and dApp interactions should feel familiar. What changes is the ecosystem, the apps, and the community culture around the chain.
Bridge assets into Gorbagana Cash, then visit the ecosystem page to find the dApps you want to use. Swap, explore, mint, build, or just watch the cookie jar fill up.
Simple path
Bridge in. Pick a dApp. Use GCASH like a Solana-style network with a different ecosystem.

title: Cookie Chain — COOK Native Token
content:
Cookie Chain — COOK (Native Token)

COOK is the native asset of Cookie Chain. It pays network fees, secures the chain through validator stake, and serves as the primary unit of account across the ecosystem.

Key facts:
- Symbol: COOK
- Decimals: 9 (SPL and lamports-style precision)
- Fee currency: all network fees are paid in COOK
- Bridge model: COOK is bridged 1:1 to its Solana counterpart through the community multi-sig

How to get COOK:
- Bridge from Solana via bridge.cookiescan.io
- Swap for COOK on ecosystem DEXs
- Receive COOK directly from any Cookie Chain wallet

Only use bridges and RPC endpoints linked from official community channels. There is no centralized foundation — verify endpoints before signing.

title: Cookie Jar — Community Treasury Vault
content:
Cookie Jar — Community Treasury Vault

Cookie Jar is the name given to the second community multisig vault (Vault 1). It collects ecosystem-aligned donations and routes assets toward builders, developers, and contributors working on Cookie Chain.

Cookie Jar Vault 1 address: 568tU9FMksJDxjkLBjWisSA4J4C5uPH87NCCkyREwrxe

Who controls it: the community multisig — not a single operator, founder, or private wallet.

What donations are for:
- Builder and developer incentives
- Grants for useful apps, tools, and infrastructure
- Support for contributors shipping public goods
- Funding experiments that grow the Cookie Chain ecosystem

Leading Cookie Chain ecosystem projects can donate a portion of their revenue into Cookie Jar as voluntary ecosystem contributions.

title: Cookie Chain — For Degens (Quick Start)
content:
Cookie Chain — For Degens

A fast, safer path for trading, bridging, exploring apps, and moving through the ecosystem.

Degen quick path:
1. Install Nightly wallet (nightly.app) and add the Cookie Chain RPC.
2. Bridge COOK through bridge.cookiescan.io
3. Track balances, transactions, blocks, and validators on cookiescan.io
4. Explore live programs and ecosystem dApps from cookiescan.io/programs

Before you ape:
- Verify bridge, RPC, and app URLs before signing.
- Use a hot wallet for daily dApp activity and a separate wallet for storage.
- Keep enough COOK for fees before interacting with programs.
- Check explorer activity and program pages before trusting a new app.

Fast path: Wallets → COOK → Bridge → Ecosystem Programs
